coredata.dc:title: Development of a pH-responsive ratiometric fluoroprobe based on GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+ and N-CQDs for rapid/sensitive malathion detection in fruit juices
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Mei, He
Huang, Jingcheng
Wang, Guixin
Chen, Kun
Wang, Huili
Wang, Xuedong
Wang, Wenwei
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we engineered a novel pH-responsive ratiometric assay by virtue of the composite system of Ce3+-enhanced glutathione-encapsulated copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+ and N-CQDs were fabricated using one-pot chemical reduction and hydrothermal approaches, respectively, and their morphologies as well as physical and chemical properties were testified in detail by a series of characterization techniques. At 350 nm excitation wavelength, the GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+/N-CQDs system (GCC-NC) featured the 440/650 nm dual-emitting property. The ratios of fluorescence intensities (F650/F440) demonstrated a strong pH-dependence from 3.0 to 5.0 with a coefficient of determination of 0.9982. Consequently, the GCC-NC system was feasible for constructing a pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe. Based on the catalytic effect of acetylcholinesterase on acetylcholine to yield acetic acid and further trigger varying solution pH, the as-constructed GCC-NC fluoroprobe was satisfactorily applied for detecting malathion. Under optimized conditions, the newly developed GCC-NC fluoroprobe supplied a wider linear range (0.25–200 μM), lower detection limit (0.075 μM), satisfactory recoveries (96.3–106.2%) and higher precision for malathion in several kinds of fruit juice samples. The fortified experiments by malathion’s structural analogues and metal ions provided compelling evidence that this fluoroprobe had strong anti-interference capacity and high specificity for malathion. These findings render us to believe that the as-constructed pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe holds great promise in trace malathion assay in food matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acetylcholinesterase’s catalytic effect
Ce3+-enhanced copper nanoclusters
Malathion
N-doped carbon quantum dots
pH-responsive ratiometric assay

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the GSH-CuNCs and N-CQDs
Construction of a pH-responsive fluorescence platform for malathion based on the mixed system of GCC-NC
Characterization of the GSH-CuNCs and N-CQDs
pH responses of the ratiometric probe based on the GCC-NC mixed system
pH responses of the ratiometric probe based on the GCC-NC for the detection of malathion
Selectivity of the pH-responsive GCC-NC dual-emitting fluoroprobe
Comparison of the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe with previous methods
Detection of malathion in actual samples by the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was jointly sponsored by the Natural Science Foundation of China (22076134), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LY19B070010, LQ20B070009 and LQ21B050004), Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (BK20211338), Key Sci&Technol Project of Suzhou City (SS202028) and South Zhejiang Institute of Radiation Medicine and Nuclear Technology (ZFY-2022-K-004).

This work was jointly sponsored by the Natural Science Foundation of China (22076134), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LY19B070010, LQ20B070009 and LQ21B050004), Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (BK20211338), Key Sci&Technol Project of Suzhou City (SS202028), Jiangsu Provincial Key R&D Project (BE2022733), and South Zhejiang Institute of Radiation Medicine and Nuclear Technology (ZFY-2022-K-004).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), FT-IR spectrum (b), XPS survey spectrum (c), and fluorescent spectrum of N-CQDs.
TEM image (a), FT-IR spectrum (b), XPS survey spectrum (c), and fluorescent spectrum of GSH-CuNCs.
(a) The effects of solution pH on FIs of the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe; (b) The linear correlation between the F650/F440 and solution pH.
Optimization of Ce3+ concentration (a) and AChE concentration (b); Optimization of the temperature (c) and time (d) for the reaction between AChE and ATCh.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of the GCC-NC fluoroprobe; and (b) Linear relationship between the F650/F440 and the concentration of malathion.
(a) Selectivity of the GCC-NC fluoroprobe towards malathion; and (b) Durable stability of the N-CQDs solution stored at 4°C.

Schematic diagram regarding the GCC-NC based pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe for malathion assay. GCC-NC denotes the abbreviation of Ce3+-enhanced glutathione-encapsulated copper nanoclusters and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots.
Comparison of the GCC-NC fluoroprobe with previous methods for malathion assay.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: LRs, LODs, and RRs denote the acronyms of linear ranges, limits of detection and relative recoveries, respectively. GCC-NC represents the abbreviation of Ce3+-enhanced glutathione-encapsulated copper nanoclusters and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots. SERs, surface-enhanced Raman spectrometry; AuNCs, gold nanoclusters; AChE, acetylcholinesterase; BChE/AgNWs/PTTBO, Butyrylcholinesterase/Siver nanowires/Poly(5,6-bis(octyloxy)-4,7- di(thieno[3][3,2-b]thiophen-2-yl)benzo[c][1,2,5]oxoadiazole) (PTTBO); MG/KIO4, Malachite green/potassium peroidate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical performance of the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe for malathion assay in fruit juices.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: (1) RRs and RSD% are the acronyms of relative recoveries and relative standard deviations, respectively; and (2) * indicate that the differences of concentration detected by the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe based GCC-NC and gas chromatography (GC) (SN/T 0131–2010) were not statistically significant (P>0.05); and (3) △means the slop of the relationship between estimated and actual concentrations.

head.ce:title: Development of a pH-responsive ratiometric fluoroprobe based on GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+ and N-CQDs for rapid/sensitive malathion detection in fruit juices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou 215009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: South Zhejiang Institute of Radiation Medicine and Nuclear Technology, Wenzhou 325809, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: South Zhejiang Institute of Radiation Medicine and Nuclear Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: South Zhejiang Institute of Radiation Medicine and Nuclear Technology, Wenzhou 325809, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: College of Public Health, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325035, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Public Health, Wenzhou Medical University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: He Mei and Jingcheng Huang contributed equally to this work and shared the first authorship.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we engineered a novel pH-responsive ratiometric assay by virtue of the composite system of Ce3+-enhanced glutathione-encapsulated copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+ and N-CQDs were fabricated using one-pot chemical reduction and hydrothermal approaches, respectively, and their morphologies as well as physical and chemical properties were testified in detail by a series of characterization techniques. At 350 nm excitation wavelength, the GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+/N-CQDs system (GCC-NC) featured the 440/650 nm dual-emitting property. The ratios of fluorescence intensities (F650/F440) demonstrated a strong pH-dependence from 3.0 to 5.0 with a coefficient of determination of 0.9982. Consequently, the GCC-NC system was feasible for constructing a pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe. Based on the catalytic effect of acetylcholinesterase on acetylcholine to yield acetic acid and further trigger varying solution pH, the as-constructed GCC-NC fluoroprobe was satisfactorily applied for detecting malathion. Under optimized conditions, the newly developed GCC-NC fluoroprobe supplied a wider linear range (0.25–200 μM), lower detection limit (0.075 μM), satisfactory recoveries (96.3–106.2%) and higher precision for malathion in several kinds of fruit juice samples. The fortified experiments by malathion’s structural analogues and metal ions provided compelling evidence that this fluoroprobe had strong anti-interference capacity and high specificity for malathion. These findings render us to believe that the as-constructed pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe holds great promise in trace malathion assay in food matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) have relatively high activity and low synthetic cost, and thus they are extensively utilized in agricultural and forestry production to control insects and fungi (Li et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2021). Malathion is a widely used OPs, which has adverse effects on humans by means of irreversibly suppressing acetylcholinesterase’s (AChE’s) activity (Solla et al., 2011). When the AChE’s activity is prominently suppressed by malathion, the accumulated phenomenon of acetylcholine (ACh) will appear in neurotransmitters and further cause a series of neurological diseases and even death (Lee and Lee, 2011). Fruit juice drink is one of the most popular beverages in our daily life. However, plant materials (apple, peach, grape, etc.) used in beverage production may be contaminated by malathion, which may lead to trace-level residual pollution of beverage products. To ensure the safety of our daily consumed beverages, the development of simple, convenient, and sensitive methodologies for malathion assay is crucial for protecting human health.

So far, several types of methodologies have been engineered/developed for malathion assay, such as gas/liquid chromatography (Lee and Lee, 2011; Qian et al., 2017), mass spectrometry (Borras et al., 2011) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Qian et al. 2009) and so on. In spite of these traditional methodologies possess high detection sensitivity and precision, they need precise/expensive instruments, complex pre-treatment procedures, high cost, and operation of professional technicians, and also have technical defects, such as time-consuming and unsuitable application under field conditions. In order to address the aforementioned problems, it still need to design and develop novel approaches for rapid/convenient OPs detection. To date, several types of biosensors based on enzymatic catalysis reaction have been adopted for OPs assay, among which AChE, alkaline phosphatase and tyrosinase are highly preferred (Yan et al., 2015; Lv et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2017). Building upon the aforementioned enzymes, many assay methodologies have been developed on the basis of their combination with biological fluoroprobe, electrochemical sensing, and colorimetric assay (Long et al., 2015; Sgobbi and Machado, 2018; Wang et al., 2019). Among these sensing methods, fluoroprobes are widely employed in several fields, such as environmental monitoring and biochemical analysis due to their high assay sensitivity, low preparation cost, and relatively simple operating procedures.

The dual-emitting fluoroprobe, also known as ratiometric fluoroprobe, is engineered and improved on the basis of the single-emitting fluoroprobe. Compared to a single-emitting signal, the dual-emitting fluorescence signal can significantly reduce the interference caused by a series of external factors, such as temperature, humidity, and varying concentrations of the fluorescent material itself (Yang, et al., 2020). In addition, the reference fluorescence signal often functions as a correction variable in the dual-emitting fluoroprobe, thereby greatly enhancing the stability of the detection results (Li et al., 2020). Ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobes enable a high signal-to-background ratio for improved accuracy and higher detection sensitivity (Huang et al., 2018). Moreover, the ratio of two fluorescent signals will only be related to the analyte, free from the environmental effects, leading to high anti-inferring capacity (Bigdeli et al., 2019). To enhance the feasibility and extendibility of fluorescent analysis, the researches on dual-emitting fluoroprobes have attracted plentiful eyes in recent years (Han et al., 2020). Wang’s teamwork anchored silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) onto the surface of SiO2 spheres to fabricate dual-emitting nanohybrids for the ratiometric fluorescence assay of S2- (Wen et al., 2019a). Wen’s team pioneered a dual-emitting fluoroprobe based on the combination of CuNCs and carbon dots (CDs) for the assay of H2S (Wen et al., 2019b). An innovative ratiometric fluoroprobe was engineered utilizing the composite system of boron/nitride co-doped CDs and Eu3+ for sensitive detection of tetracyclines in milk (Yang et al., 2020). However, these dual-emitting ratiometric fluoroprobes focus on the reaction of metal ions with target analytes, and no pH-dependent dual-emitting fluoroprobe is designed and developed for detecting analytes. As such, it is innovative and still in the initial stage for the development of pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobes.

Among three metal nanoclusters (AgNCs, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and CuNCs), CuNCs possess the advantages of the lowest cost and most stable signal, and thus hold great promise in construction of the chemical/biological sensor along with the cell-imaging field owing to their simple and convenient synthetic procedures (Gao et al., 2020; Zhuang et al., 2020). Ma’s group determined o-phenylenediamine in several types of water samples by employing the blue fluorescence of CuNCs at 432 nm under the 334 nm excitation condition (Ma et al., 2019). Sahu and coworkers pioneered the surfactant-free CuNCs without the addition of external stabilizers for the detection of vitamin C (Sahu et al., 2019). Our group (2021) first observed that Ce3+ substantially improved the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) phenomenon of GSH-CuNCs with a 42-fold fluorescence-enhancing effect (Mei et al., 2021, Zhang et al., 2021). However, the main drawbacks of these single-emitting fluoroprobes lie in that they are easily interfered by many experimental parameters, such as matrix effect, external light source, and non-target analytes.

Carbon dots are spherical carbon materials with a size of ∼10 nm, which have excellent water solubility, a green synthesis route, and excellent optical properties (Shamsipur et al., 2017; Semeniuk et al., 2019). Nowadays, CDs have been extensively utilized as fluorescence-sensing components for the rapid assay of organic/inorganic analytes in environmental/food matrices (Huang et al., 2020). They attract plentiful eyes owing to their prominent advantages, such as simple preparation, low toxicity, and cost, as well as biocompatibility (Thakkar et al., 2019). Chen and colleagues prepared amphiphilic CDs for trinitrophenol detection with a detectable concentration of 0.23 μM (Chen et al., 2016). Wang’s group (2020) engineered a CDs-based composite fluorescence sensor (MCOFs@MIPs@CDs) and acquired a satisfactory detection limit of 0.1 µM for trinitrophenol in waters (Wang et al., 2020). To improve the fluorescent properties of CDs, heteroatom doping, especially nitrogen element (N), has aroused more attentions. For example, a probe based on N-doped CQDs was prepared using uric acid as a C/N source via a one-step pyrolysis and used to construct a fluorescent nanosensor for colchicine assay based on the inner filter effect (El-Malla et al., 2021).

In general, these above-mentioned CuNCs or CDs-based fluorescent methods are simple and convenient, but their sensitivity and anti-inferring capacity are far from satisfactory. To address the above issue, this study aims to combine the Ce3+-enhanced GSH-CuNCs (GSH-CuNCs/Ce3+, GCC) with N-dopped carbon-quantum dots (N-CQDs, NC) to constitute a dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescent platform for the improvement of sensitivity and anti-interference characteristics. The resulting fluoroprobe, hereafter abbreviated as GCC-NC, exhibits dual-emitting peaks (blue at 440 nm and red at 650 nm) at a 350 nm single excitation. As we know, the residues of OPs can significantly suppress the activity of AChE, while AChE catalyzes the production of acetic acid from ACh, and the formation of acetic acid will lead to inevitable pH changes in aqueous solution, thereby affecting the response of a pH-dependent fluoroprobe. These phenomena are conducive to establishing a pH-oriented fluorescence system for the assay of OPs (Yan et al., 2015). By employing the solution pH changes in the AChE/ACh catalytic reaction, the dual-emitting GCC-NC fluoroprobe provides a sensitive fluorescence assay of trace-level malathion in fruit juices ( Scheme 1) and holds great promise in food safety monitoring of malathion.

The chemicals used in this investigation were of analytical grade and procured from Aladdin (Shanghai, China), including copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5 H2O), glutathione (GSH), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetylcholinesterase (AChE), sodium citrate monohydrate, acetylthiocholine (ATCh, an analogue of acetylcholine), malathion, sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4·7 H2O), triphenyl phosphate (TPP), triethyl phosphate (TEP), tris(chloroisopropyl) propyl) phosphate (TCPP) and tris(2,3-dichloropropyl) phosphate (TDCPP). When received, the analytical-grade chemicals were used directly without any purification. Ultrapure water (H2O) with electrical conductivity of ≥18.2 MΩ·cm was produced by a Milli-Q system (Bedford, MA, USA) and employed in all experimental processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the GSH-CuNCs and N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A facile one-pot reduction method was employed to synthesize GSH-CuNCs according to the previous approach reported by Wang and his coworkers (Han, et al., 2020). First, aliquots (10.0 mL) of GSH solution (50 mg/mL) and 10.0 mL of CuSO4·5 H2O solution (10 mM) were homogeneously mixed and stirred at 37°C for 1 h. Next, an appropriate volume of NaOH solution (0.5 M) was fortified into the above homogeneous solution until the clear solution turned to light yellow, which remained stirring for 2 h to obtain the GSH-CuNCs solution. The 50-mL flask containing the resulting solution of GSH-CuNCs was wrapped with aluminum foil and placed at 4°C for further trials.

As for the fabrication of N-CQDs, sodium citrate monohydrate (0.2 g), NH4Cl (1.1 g), and H2O (10 mL) were homogeneously mixed until complete dissolution of these precursors, and then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180°C for 4 h (Wen et al., 2019a). After cooling at ambient conditions, the resultant reactants were transferred, and the mixture was purified overnight with a dialysis membrane (5000 Da) in a dark environment. Subsequently, the obtained product was further purified after a 5-min centrifugation at 5000 rpm to remove insoluble impurities. Finally, the N-CQDs solution obtained was sealed, wrapped with aluminum foil, and placed at 4°C for further experiments.

A Talos F200S (Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to characterize the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the GSH-CuNCs and N-CQDs. A Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi XPS (MA, USA) was used to analyze the elemental components in the GSH-CuNCs and N-CQDs. The FT-IR spectra of the GSH-CuNCs and N-CQDs were conducted on a Thermo Fisher Scientific IS5. Fluorescent emission spectra were measured on an Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer (London, England). Solution pH was tested using a Leici PBSJ-6 L model pH meter (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument, Shanghai, China). The UV–vis absorption and zeta potential were recorded on a PERSEE TU-1901 (Pgeneral, Beijing, China) and a Zetasizer NanoZS90 (Malvern Instruments, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a pH-responsive fluorescence platform for malathion based on the mixed system of GCC-NC
ce:section.ce:para: The GCC-NC fluorescence system was prepared by mixing 200 μL of GSH-CuNCs, 100 μL of N-CQDs and aliquots of Ce3+ solution in 500 μL of acetic acid-sodium acetate (HAc-NaAc) buffer, and incubated in a water-bath thermostat for 10 min. Then, the solutions of AChE (100 μL, 40 μM), malathion (200 μL) with varying concentrations, and HAc-NaAc buffer (1500 μL) were homogenously mixed and kept it for 10 min. After that, the pH of the above mixed solution was adjusted to 5.0 using 0.5 M HCl, and ATCh (200 μL, 100 mM) was added to the solution and kept in a water-bath thermostat for 30 min at 37°C to obtain the reaction solution. Before spectral analysis, 500 μL of the GCC-NC solution and 500 μL of the reaction solution were mixed in a 2-mL centrifuge tube and remained still for 10 min. Fluorescence spectra of the GCC-NC system were determined at the 350 nm excitation wavelength. The ratios of fluorescence signals at 650 nm and 440 nm emission wavelengths (F650/F440) were considered as an evaluation indicator for the analytical performance of the GCC-NC probe.

Five kinds of fruit juice beverages were collected from Suzhou Dayunfa Supermarket (Suzhou, China). The collected juice samples were first filtered with filter papers to remove large particulate matter and further purified by 0.45-µm membrane to remove insoluble substances. After the purified aqueous suspension was adjusted to pH 5.0 using 0.5 M HCl, 200 μL of the above suspension was added to the mixed system of GCC-NC, AChE, and ATCh as described in Section 2.4. After a 30-min incubation at 37°C, the F650/F440 value was measured when the system was excited at 350 nm. The fortification recoveries for malathion by the GCC-NC fluoroprobe were investigated by spiking malathion at varying concentrations into the mixed fluorescent system.

In fruit juices, there exists a wide range of interfering chemicals/ions to affect the fluorescent signals of as-constructed GCC-NC fluoroprobe. To appraise the selectivity of this dual-emitting fluoroprobe to malathion, some common ions and structural analogues were chosen as the representative interference species, including Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, CO3 2-, NO3 -, SO4 2- along with TCPP, TDCPP, TEP, and TPP. The same fluorescence measurement was performed as described in Section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the GSH-CuNCs and N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As elaborated in Fig. 1a, the N-CQDs displayed uneven-distribution spherical quantum dots with a size of about 4 nm by TEM, which was basically consistent with the previous reports (Wen et al., 2019b; Gao et al., 2020). By FT-IR spectrum, the N-CQDs exhibited the stretching vibrations of -CN- and -NH- bonds at 1420–1400 cm−1 and 3500–3100 cm−1 (Fig. 1b), respectively, hinting the presence of N as a dopant in the quantum dots (Wen et al., 2019a). By XPS characterization, the peaks at 285, 400, 530, and 1070 eV represented the C, N, O, and Na elements, respectively (Fig. 1c), indicating the successful doping of N atoms in the synthetic process (Zhuang et al., 2020). Furthermore, the fluorescent property of N-CQDs showed that the maximum emission wavelength of N-CQDs was observed at 440 nm when it was excited at 350 nm (Fig. 1d).

By TEM observation, no obvious aggregation phenomenon was observed for GSH-CuNCs, and they were uniformly distributed with ∼4 nm of spherical shape ( Fig. 2a), which was in general agreement with the previous documents (Ma et al., 2019; Sahu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021; Shamsipur et al., 2017). In contrast to GSH-CuNCs, the -SH stretching band at 2522 cm−1 in GSH disappeared (Fig. 2b), which demonstrated that the Cu-S covalent bonds generated. By XPS spectral analysis (Fig. 2c), the peaks at 162, 281, 400 and 536 eV confirmed that the S, C, N and O elements were present in GSH, while a new peak at 970 eV assigning to Cu element occurred for the GSH-CuNCs (Semeniuk et al., 2019). These observations higlight the successful encapsulation of CuNCs with GSH. When excited at 350 nm, the maximum emission of GSH-CuNCs was observed at 650 nm (Fig. 2d). As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescent intensity of GSH-CuNCs greatly enhanced upon the addition of Ce3+. This finding indicates that the Ce3+ can improved the fluorescence of GSH-CuNCs owing to the AIE effect induced by Ce3+, which has been also confirmed in our previous studies (Mei et al., 2021, Zhang et al., 2021).

To evaluate the interaction between the GCC and NC, UV-Vis absorption spectra and zeta potential were tested. As shown in Fig. S2a, the absorption spectrum of GCC-NC displayed a distinct blue-shift compared with GCC, while it showed an obvious red-shift compared with NC. This shift might be due to the coordination interactions between Ce3+ and amino functional groups of NC. As shown in Fig. S2b, the zeta potential of GCC was about +7.3 mV, while it decreased to −5.2 mV upon the addition of negatively charged NC (-12.6 mV). These data provide compelling evidence that strong electrostatic interactions existed between GCC and NC. Based on the above analyses, it can be inferred that the coordination and electrostatic interactions lead to the formation of GCC-NC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH responses of the ratiometric probe based on the GCC-NC mixed system
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the pH responses of GCC-NC, buffer solutions were prepared with varying pH (3.0, 3.6, 4.0, 4.6 and 5.0), and the corresponding fluorescence intensities (FIs) are exhibited in Fig. 3. Clearly, the 650 nm FIs were sharply decreased from 5.50×105 to 1.65×105 (a.u.), but the 440 nm FIs were progressively increased with the elevation of solution pH (Fig. 3a). Correspondingly, the ratiometric fluorescence signals (F650/F440) of GCC-NC changed linearly with variations in solution pH. As a consequence, a satisfactory linear equation (y=-0.8475x+4.9665) was achieved between the F650/F440 and solution pH with coefficient of determination (R2) up to 0.9982 and P<0.001 (Fig. 3b). Building upon the aforementioned phenomenon, we posit that the newly developed fluoroprobe, based on the combination of GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+ with N-CQDs, is feasible for constructing a pH-responsive dual-emitting fluorescence platform. To confirm the practical application capability of the assay, the standard solutions (pH 4.0) were selected as real samples. The differences between the results detected by the assay based GCC-NC (3.9) and the level of the standard solution (pH 4.0) were not statistically significant (P>0.05), indicating that the proposed assay exhibited outstanding practical application capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH responses of the ratiometric probe based on the GCC-NC for the detection of malathion
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of constructing a highly efficient fluorescence platform, we optimized the Ce3+ concentration in the GCC-NC system. The elevating Ce3+ concentrations from 0.1 to 10.0 mM led to dramatic rises in the F650/F440 values, i.e., the ratio of the 650 nm FIs (F650) of GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+ to the 440 nm FIs (F440) of N-CQDs ( Fig. 4a). However, when the concentrations of Ce3+ further increased from 10 to 40 mM, the F650/F440 values remained nearly unchanged (Fig. 4a). Thus, 10 mM of Ce3+ was fortified in the following experiments. Subsequently, 100 μL of AChE at varying concentrations (0–80 μM) and 200 μL of ATCh (100 mM) were sequentially introduced into 100 μL of the GCC-NC composite system, and the final solution was dissolved to 1.0 mL with HAc-NaAc buffer. After that, the reaction solution was shaken at 37°C for 30 min for spectral analysis. At 100 mM of ATCh, the concentration of AChE was investigated over the range of 0–80 μM. Clearly, the F650/F440 values sharply increased from 0.68 to 2.12 when the fortified concentrations varied from 0 to 40 μM. Surprisingly, further increases in the AChE concentrations from 40 to 80 μM caused almost constant fluorescence signal (Fig. 4b). Building upon the above observations, 40 μM was selected as the appropriate concentration of AChE in subsequent trials. As shown in Fig. 4c, the F650/F440 values reached the maximum when the temperature of the reaction solution between ATCh and AChE was set at 37°C. Simultaneously, the F650/F440 values increased gradually with the increasing reaction time and reached a plateau at 30 min (Fig. 4d). Thus, the optimized time and temperature of the reaction between ATCh and AChE were set at 37°C and 30 min, respectively, which were in agreement with previous reports (Yan et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2019).

Under optimized conditions, the assay performance of the pH-responsive GCC-NC system was evaluated in aspects of analytical metrics, including linear range (LR), detection limit (LOD), quantification limit (LOQ), and R2. When malathion was spiked from 0 to 200 μM to the GCC-NC system, a progressive downward F650/F440 values were clearly observed in concomitant with the solution color turning from bright red to dark blue (insert of Fig. 5a). Based on the above responses, a good LR was obtained from 0.25 to 200 μM with a linear equation: y=-0.4471x+1.7592 (R2= 0.9965, P<0.001) (Fig. 5b). According to the signal-to-noise of 3 and 10 (S/N=3 and 10), the LOD and LOQ of the pH-responsive fluoroprobe were estimated to be 0.075 μM and 0.25 μM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the pH-responsive GCC-NC dual-emitting fluoroprobe
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of evaluating the selectivity of the pH-responsive GCC-NC dual-emitting fluoroprobe, chemicals with similar structural or optical properties as malathion were selected as interfering substances, including TCPP, TDCPP, TEP, and TPP, which remain stable in acidic or neutral conditions and are easily hydrolyzed in alkaline conditions (Barnard et al., 1966; Bueno et al., 2021). Additionally, several common metal ions were also adopted to analyze their interfering effects (Huang et al., 2020; Thakkar et al., 2019), which were composed of Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, NO3-, CO3 - , and SO4 2-. Thus, TCPP, TDCPP, TEP, TPP, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, NaNO3, MgSO4, and ZnSO4 were selected as interferences to examine the selectivity of the proposed assay based on GCC-NC. It can be seen from Fig. 6a that the F650/F440 values by interfering ions/chemicals were all <0.30 but reached as high as ∼2.50 upon the addition of malathion. Although the fortified levels were 10 times as high as that of malathion, other potential interfering chemicals and ions had negligible effects on the dual-emitting signals of the GCC-NC mixed system, reflecting in that all the solution color was red except for the addition of malathion (insert of Fig. 6a). These observations render us to believe that the pH-responsive fluoroprobe, consisting of the composite system of GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+ and N-CQDs, has excellent selectivity for malathion.

Besides, when the GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+ system was stored at 4°C for 60 days, no changes in the 650 nm emission were observed. Similarly, the FIs of N-CQDs hardly changed after a 60 days storage at 4°C (Fig. 6b), which substantiated the high stability of as-fabricated nanomaterial. To evaluate the stability of the proposed material based on GCC-NC, the values of F650/F440 of the freshly prepared fluoroprobe solutions were tested within three weeks. As shown in Fig.S3, the values of F650/F440 kept 97.2% of the initial value after three weeks of storage at room temperature. This result indicated that the proposed material possessed prominent stability.

Furthermore, the repeatability of the proposed assay based on GCC-NC for malathion detection is shown in Fig. S4a, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs%) of three-parallel assay detected in the same laboratory by the same one toward malathion (1, 10 and 200 μM) were <4.0%, testifying a excellent repeatability of the assay. For the stability of the proposed assay for malathion detection, the RSDs% obtained in five different laboratories by the different people toward 200 μM malathion was 5.6%, demonstrating the outstanding stability of the proposed assay (Fig. S4b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe with previous methods
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 exhibits the advantages of the pH-responsive GCC-NC fluoroprobe in contrast to other previous approaches. Besides the conventional chromatographic methods, other methodologies have been designed/developed for OPs assay, such as snowflake-like AuNCs/SERS (Huang et al., 2020), AChE/carbon paste electrode/voltammetry (Caetano, Machado, 2008), BChE/AgNWs/PTTBO/graphite/ voltammetry (Turan et al., 2016), MG/KIO4/kinetic spectrometry (Mitic et al., 2012), and animal waste biomass CDs/fluorescence detection (Liu et al., 2020). The LOD (0.075 μM) offered by the GCC-NC fluoroprobe is substantially lower than those (0.21–5.4 μM) by AChE/carbon paste electrode/voltammetry (Caetano, Machado, 2008), BChE/AgNw/PTTBO/graphite/voltammetry (Turan et al., 2016) and MG/KIO4/kinetic spectrometry (Mitic et al., 2012). Comparatively, it is similar to those (0.064–0.10 μM) by snowflake-like Au NCs/SERS (Huang et al., 2020) and animal waste biomass CDs/fluorescence detection (Liu et al., 2020). Significantly, the as-contructed ratiometric probe offers wider LRs (0.25–200 μM) for OPs than the above-mentioned methods (Huang et al., 2020; Caetano, Machado, 2008; Turan et al., 2016; Mitic et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2020). Moreover, it provides more satisfactory recoveries (96.3–106.2%) than those (78.3–83.4%) by AChE/carbon paste electrode/voltammetry (Caetano, Machado, 2008), and similar recoveries as other approaches (Huang et al., 2020; Turan et al., 2016; Mitic et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2020). The detection time is 50 min for the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe based on GCC-NC, which is longer than the above-mentioned methods (10 s to 15 min) (Huang et al., 2020; Caetano, Machado, 2008; Turan et al., 2016; Mitic et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2020). As a consequence, it can be concluded that this pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe provides an innovative way for malathion residual detection in fruit juices, as well as applications in other complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of malathion in actual samples by the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibilityand extendability of this pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe, the fortified recoveries for malathion were investigated in a series of fruit juices, including peach, pear, cranberry, plum, and strawberry juices, and honey grapefruit tea. As generalized in Table 2, the residues of malathion in all samples were detected to be less than its corresponding LOD (0.075 μM). At three fortification levels (5, 10, and 50 μM), there were no statistically significant differences (P>0.05) between the results detected by the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe and that by gas chromatography (GC) (SN/T 0131–2010). What’s more, the slopes of the linearity of the relationship between estimated and actual concentrations spanned the range from 0.9810 to 1.0047. The recoveries for malathion varied between 96.3% and 106.2% with RSD% <3.8%. These results indicated that the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe showed excellent accuracy for malathion detection in juice matrices. Moreover, the intra-day and inter-day precision, which was recorded as RSDs% (n=6), varied between 1.4% and 4.2%. These superior performance indexes let us conclude that this pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe has excellent potential and holds great promise in the residual detection of malathion in juice matrices.

In this investigation, a pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe, based on the composite system of GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+ and N-CQDs, was engineered and developed for trace malathion assay in fruit juices. The fabrication of GSH-CuNCs-Ce3+ and N-CQDs was based on the facile one-pot reduction and hydrothermal methods, respectively. The introduction of Ce3+ initiated the AIE phenomenon of GSH-CuNCs and greatly enhanced the FIs of the composite system. At 350 nm excitation, the GCC-NC composite system exhibited dual-emitting peaks at 440 and 650 nm. A strong linear response was constructed between the F650/F440 values and solution pH from 3.0 to 5.0. Therefore, this GCC-NC composite system was feasible for constructing a pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe. Based on AChE’s inhibitory effect, this dual-emitting fluoroprobe realized the specific assay of malathion in fruit juice samples, showing rapid response and strong anti-interference capacity. Moreover, the GCC-NC fluoroprobe supplied low LOD, wide LR, and high precision. The limitation of the pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe based on GCC-NC is that the optimum incubation time of AChE and ATCh is too long, leading to a longer detection time. Building upon these observations, it can be posited that this pH-responsive dual-emitting fluoroprobe has potential application prospects for residual monitoring of malathion in food matrices.

Kun Chen: Formal analysis, Data curation. Huili Wang: Project administration, Methodology. He Mei: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Guixin Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Wenwei Wang: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Jingcheng Huang: Writing – review & editing. Xuedong Wang: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was jointly sponsored by the Natural Science Foundation of China (22076134), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LY19B070010, LQ20B070009 and LQ21B050004), Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (BK20211338), Key Sci&Technol Project of Suzhou City (SS202028), Jiangsu Provincial Key R&D Project (BE2022733), and South Zhejiang Institute of Radiation Medicine and Nuclear Technology (ZFY-2022-K-004).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.106225.
